scenarios.title: Interview Time!
scenarios.description: You are interviewing Arvind for the Admin Manager position. Arvind is wearing a bindi but also has facial hair and is wearing a shirt and pants. Their name does not indicate their gender identity.
scenarios.question: What do you do?

options.text: Ask about their education and start the conversation.
options.feedback: This is a safe approach but might seem like you are avoiding the topic of gender identity. A more inclusive way is to lead by example—introduce yourself with your name and pronouns.

options.text: Introduce yourself with your name and pronouns (e.g., "Hi, I'm Ramu, and I use he/him pronouns").
options.feedback: Excellent choice! By sharing your pronouns, you create a safe space for Arvind to share theirs if they choose to.

options.text: Ask if they use she/her pronouns.
options.feedback: It's good that you want to be respectful, but asking directly can feel invasive. It's better to lead by example by sharing your own pronouns first.

scenarios.title: Washroom Concerns
scenarios.description: Amala, a senior manager, questions why transgender women are allowed to use the women's washroom despite having a gender-inclusive washroom.
scenarios.question: What do you do?

options.text: Explain to Amala that transgender women are women and deserve to be treated with respect and dignity.
options.feedback: Great response! True inclusivity respects individuals' identities and ensures they are treated with dignity.

options.text: Agree to disagree because you feel uncomfortable discussing it.
options.feedback: Avoiding the conversation does not resolve the issue. It's important to address concerns with empathy and accurate information.

options.text: Sternly tell Amala that she needs to learn about transgender rights.
options.feedback: While Amala might need more awareness, approaching the conversation with empathy fosters understanding and acceptance more effectively.

scenarios.title: Out or About?
scenarios.description: You overhear Priya discussing the sexual orientation of another employee without their consent.
scenarios.explainer: Outing is revealing someone's LGBTQ+ identity without their consent, potentially impacting their safety, relationships, or professional life.
scenarios.question: What do you do?

options.text: Approach Priya privately and explain the importance of respecting others' privacy.
options.feedback: Excellent choice! This approach maintains confidentiality and educates Priya on respecting personal boundaries.

options.text: Ignore the conversation since it wasn't meant maliciously.
options.feedback: Ignoring the situation perpetuates a culture of gossip and invasion of privacy. It's crucial to address such matters respectfully.

options.text: Confront Priya in front of others to make a point about respect.
options.feedback: While it's important to address the issue, public confrontation can lead to defensiveness. Private conversations are more effective for learning and growth.

scenarios.title: Hire! Hire!
scenarios.description: Your team is hiring for a leadership role. One candidate, Tanvi, is from the LGBTIAQ+ community and another, Arjun, is a cisgender man. Both have equal qualifications.
scenarios.explainer: Cisgender refers to someone whose gender identity matches the sex they were assigned at birth.
scenarios.question: What do you do?

options.text: Choose Tanvi to promote diversity.
options.feedback: While promoting diversity is important, decisions should be based on qualifications, skills, and the value they bring to the team, rather than identity alone.

options.text: Choose the candidate who best aligns with the team's needs, regardless of identity.
options.feedback: Perfect! This approach ensures equal opportunity without bias. Diversity is about inclusivity, not tokenism.

options.text: Seek feedback from the team about which candidate they feel comfortable working with.
options.feedback: Choosing candidates based on comfort levels can lead to biases. Decisions should be made on merit and fit for the role.

scenarios.title: Employee Resource Groups
scenarios.description: Your company is setting up an LGBTIAQ+ Employee Resource Group. Some employees express discomfort, stating they don't see the need for "special groups."
scenarios.explainer: Employee Resource Groups are voluntary, employee-led groups that foster a diverse, inclusive workplace aligned with organisational goals. They promote inclusion, raise awareness about LGBTQ+ issues, and advocate for equitable policies.
scenarios.question: What do you do?

options.text: Explain the importance of safe spaces and community support.
options.feedback: Great response! ERGs provide a sense of belonging and support, improving workplace culture and productivity.

options.text: Cancel the idea to avoid controversy.
options.feedback: Avoiding initiatives due to discomfort undermines inclusion. It's important to educate and sensitise employees on the benefits of diversity.

options.text: Implement the ERG without addressing the concerns.
options.feedback: While creating ERGs is positive, addressing concerns with empathy helps build acceptance and understanding.

scenarios.title: Reporting Harassment Without Gender-Inclusive POSH Policy
scenarios.description: Sundar, an employee who identifies as non-binary, reports harassment at work. You realise that your company's POSH (Prevention of Sexual Harassment) policy only addresses gender harassment for women, not gender minorities.
scenarios.explainer: In India, anti-discrimination policies should cover all gender identities and sexual orientations to ensure a safe and respectful workplace. The POSH Act can be updated to include protections for LGBTIAQ+ employees and also be gender inclusive to include women, men, gender non-binary persons.
scenarios.question: What do you do?

options.text: Assure Sundar of your support and investigate the complaint under existing POSH, even if it's not gender-inclusive.
options.feedback: This is a good first step, but it's a temporary solution. POSH policies need to be gender-inclusive to protect all employees, regardless of identity.

options.text: Tell Sundar that the policy doesn't cover non-binary identities but you will speak to the perpetrator informally.
options.feedback: This undermines the seriousness of the issue and fails to protect Sundar. Gender-inclusive policies are crucial for formal resolution.

options.text: Immediately initiate an update to the POSH policy to be gender-inclusive and ensure Sundar feels safe at work.
options.feedback: Excellent choice! This approach addresses the immediate issue while also taking a long-term step towards inclusion.

scenarios.title: Chosen Name vs. Dead Name
scenarios.description: Samaira, who recently transitioned, requests to use her chosen name in all official records. However, some colleagues continue using her dead name, either out of habit or ignorance.
scenarios.explainer: Transition in gender is the process of aligning one's gender identity with their outward appearance, behaviour, and/or physical body. A dead name is a person's birth name that they no longer use after transitioning to their true identity. A chosen name is the name someone selects that aligns with their gender identity, replacing their birth or dead name.
scenarios.question: What do you do?

options.text: Send out a general email reminding everyone to use correct names without specifically mentioning Samaira.
options.feedback: A good start, but this might not effectively address the issue. It's important to sensitise and educate employees on the importance of respecting chosen names.

options.text: Speak to the colleagues privately, explaining why using Samaira's chosen name is important for respect and inclusion.
options.feedback: Perfect! Educating and sensitising individuals directly promotes understanding and respect for identity.

options.text: Tell Samaira that it will take time for people to adjust, and she should be patient.
options.feedback: This minimises Samaira's experience and places the burden on her. It's the responsibility of the workplace to enforce respectful practices.

scenarios.title: Stereotyping Transgender Women as Aggressive
scenarios.description: Chitra, a transgender woman, is described as "too assertive" in her performance review, while similar behaviour is praised in her male colleagues.
scenarios.explainer: Stereotyping transgender women as aggressive stems from societal biases. Performance feedback should be objective and free of gender stereotypes.
scenarios.question: What do you do?

options.text: Ask the reviewers to reassess the feedback, highlighting potential biases.
options.feedback: Great choice! This encourages reflection on biases and promotes fair assessments.

options.text: Tell Chitra to adapt her communication style to be more acceptable to the team.
options.feedback: This reinforces stereotypes and places the responsibility on Chitra instead of addressing biases.

options.text: Ignore the feedback, assuming it's just office politics.
options.feedback: Ignoring the issue allows biases to persist, impacting Chitra's career growth and workplace experience.

scenarios.title: Lack of LGBTIAQ+ Policy Due to Low Representation
scenarios.description: Your company doesn't have an LGBTIAQ+ inclusion policy, arguing that only two employees identify as part of the community.
scenarios.question: What do you do?

options.text: Advocate for an inclusive policy, explaining that representation should not determine the need for protection and inclusion.
options.feedback: Excellent! Policies should be proactive and inclusive, ensuring safety and respect for all employees.

options.text: Agree that the policy isn't necessary, given the low numbers.
options.feedback: This approach ignores the importance of creating an inclusive workplace and discourages future employees from feeling safe to come out.

options.text: Inform the two employees privately that they can approach you if they face issues.
options.feedback: This is supportive but insufficient. Formal policies provide institutional protection and establish clear guidelines.

scenarios.title: Sensitisation on Engaging with Local Communities
scenarios.description: Your company wants to sensitise employees on interacting with local LGBTIAQ+ communities like Hijras and Kothis.
scenarios.explainer: Sensitisation involves understanding cultural contexts and challenging stereotypes. Authentic engagement promotes true inclusion.
scenarios.question: What do you do?

options.text: Organise a workshop led by community members, ensuring an authentic perspective and respectful engagement.
options.feedback: Perfect! Hearing directly from community members promotes empathy and understanding.

options.text: Circulate an email with guidelines on how to behave around Hijras and Kothis.
options.feedback: This risks stereotyping and may come across as condescending. Sensitisation requires interaction and dialogue, not just instructions.

options.text: Skip the sensitisation as most employees don't interact with these communities daily.
options.feedback: This approach ignores the importance of building an inclusive mindset, regardless of frequency of interaction.

resultLevels.message: Congratulations! You have a deep understanding of inclusion and diversity.
You're doing well! Keep learning to become a DE&I Champion.
You have a good start but need to polish your DE&I skills. Keep learning and growing.
It's time to play again and learn more about inclusive practices.
